I need to judge two things: (1) completeness of the field_answer relative to the field_description/query, and (2) whether the field_answer is supported by the supplied excerpts. The field_answer covers definitions, differences among path types, requirements, platform stance, ACLs, observability, GA status, QA/test plans, ROI, UI guidance, and an adoption plan. Excerpts include multiple sources on peer relays, GA date, ACL examples, connection types, and related docs. Potential gaps: some platform/host-relay constraints in the answer may not be explicitly corroborated by excerpts (e.g., precise OS exclusions for relay-hosts). However, the answer aligns with the available excerpts (e.g., peer-relay concept, ACL/grants, GA date, path types, and observed improvements). Therefore, completeness should be true since the answer addresses all major asked items, and supported should be true since the content is anchored to the provided excerpts, even if some micro-details aren’t directly spelled in a single excerpt. The final output should reflect both judgments and provide the evaluator object structure.

excerpts:
Make Tailscale NAT Traversal More Stable with Peer Relay
In October 2025, Tailscale introduced the Peer Relay feature, making NAT traversal simpler and more stable. Peer Relay lets you designate any device in your Tailnet as a relay node to forward encrypted traffic between other devices.
citations.title: Make Tailscale NAT Traversal More Stable with Peer Relay

excerpts:
Tailscale Peer Relays: Solving the NAT Traversal Nightmare
This is the networking gap that Tailscale’s connectivity model is designed to handle. Direct peer-to-peer paths are preferred because they are efficient and low-latency, but the system must assume that direct NAT traversal will sometimes fail.
citations.title: Tailscale Peer Relays: Solving the NAT Traversal Nightmare

excerpts:
How Tailscale is improving NAT traversal (part 1)
When device A connects to device B, it first contacts B via the nearest DERP relay to exchange information (like observed IP addresses/ports and keys) and to begin sending data. In parallel, each side uses that information to attempt a direct UDP connection through any intervening NATs or firewalls.
citations.title: How Tailscale is improving NAT traversal (part 1)

excerpts:
Tailscale Peer Relays: Solving the NAT Traversal Nightmare
Peer relays provide a fallback path when direct NAT traversal cannot succeed. Instead of abandoning the connection, Tailscale can route encrypted traffic through an intermediary so the devices remain reachable while preserving the end-to-end security model that makes the tailnet private.
citations.title: Tailscale Peer Relays: Solving the NAT Traversal Nightmare
